app.title: Inequalculator
app.tagline: How asset returns above the growth of the economy shape wealth over time
app.intro: The economy grows for everyone — but assets tend to compound faster than wages. Explore who ends up owning the growth.
app.rgLine: This is the mechanism economists call r > g: when the return on assets (r) outpaces the growth of the overall economy (g), wealth concentrates.

country.label: Country
country.help: Load real-world starting figures for each country. Every number shows its source. Editing a core slider switches to Custom.

controls.assetReturnHelp: The real, inflation-adjusted return the top group earns on its assets each year. Historically, broad stock markets have returned roughly 5–7% real over the long run.
controls.economyGrowthHelp: The real growth rate of the wider economy each year — the return applied to everyone else. This moves the tail end of the line up or down.
controls.yearHelp: Scrub through time, or press play to watch wealth compound from year 0 to the horizon.
controls.playAria: Play the animation from year 0 to the end
controls.resetAria: Reset all values to the selected country preset

stats.topShareSub: Share of all wealth owned by the top group

readout.start: At the start, the top {{top}}% owns {{share}} of all wealth.
readout.main: Everyone is {{multiple}} richer than year 0 — but the top {{top}}% now owns {{share}} of all wealth.
readout.note: Both groups are richer in absolute terms. What changes is who owns the growth.

pie.title: Who owns the wealth
pie.caption: Economy {{multiple}} larger than year 0
pie.year0Ring: Dashed ring = size of the economy at year 0
pie.a11y: Donut chart. At year {{year}}, total wealth is {{multiple}} its year-0 size. The top {{top}}% owns {{topShare}} and everyone else owns {{bottomShare}}.

divergence.title: Top group's wealth share over time
divergence.a11y: Line chart of the top {{top}}%'s share of total wealth from year 0 to year {{years}}. It starts at {{start}} and reaches {{end}} at the selected year {{year}}.
divergence.scrubHint: Tap or drag on the chart to change the year

change.title: What could change this?
change.subtitle: Add an annual wealth tax on the top group and watch the curve bend.
change.wealthTax: Wealth tax on the top group
change.wealthTaxHelp: A tax charged each year on the top group's wealth and redistributed to everyone else. Whether such a tax works in practice is debated — see the notes.
change.caveatLink: Read the caveats on wealth taxes

advanced.title: Advanced settings
advanced.subtitle: More factors, for exploring the mechanism in detail.
advanced.startingDistributionHelp: The top group's share of total wealth at year 0.
advanced.extraSavingHelp: Added to the top group's yearly growth — a proxy for higher reinvestment of returns rather than spending them.
advanced.timeHorizonHelp: How many years to project forward.

country.title: Pick a country
country.body: Load real starting figures for the US, UK or Germany — or build your own with Custom.

rates.title: Move the two rates
rates.body: Asset return (r) is what the top group earns; economy growth (g) is everyone else. When r beats g, wealth concentrates.

year.title: Travel through time
year.body: Drag the year, or press play, to watch the effect compound over decades.

info.title: Sources & assumptions
info.modelBody: Inequalculator is a stylized two-group compounding model. It splits the population into a top group (by default the top 10%) and everyone else, and compounds each group's wealth forward one year at a time using real, inflation-adjusted returns. The top group earns the asset return (r); everyone else grows with the economy (g). It is an illustration of one mechanism — it is not a forecast of any country's future.
info.rgBody: If the money you already own grows faster than the wages and output of the economy as a whole, then — all else equal — the people who own the most assets capture a growing share of total wealth over time. Thomas Piketty summarized this as r > g: the return on capital (r) exceeding the growth rate of the economy (g).
info.realReturnsNote: All returns in this tool are real (inflation-adjusted), so the growth you see is on top of inflation.

leavesOut.twoGroup: The bottom group also owns assets — homes and pensions in particular — so a clean two-group split with a single return for each side is a simplification.
leavesOut.mobility: People move between groups over their lives, and new fortunes enter the top, which a fixed two-group model does not capture.
leavesOut.empirical: Empirically, top wealth shares have risen sharply in the United States but have been comparatively flatter in the UK and Germany.
leavesOut.housing: A prominent academic view (Matthew Rognlie) attributes much of the rising capital share specifically to housing, rather than to financial capital broadly.
leavesOut.wealthTax: The effectiveness of a wealth tax is debated: valuation is hard, avoidance and capital flight are real, and estimates of the revenue it raises vary widely.

info.twoSidedNote: You can test all of this yourself by moving r, g and the wealth-tax lever and comparing countries.
info.disclaimer: An illustration of one mechanism, not a forecast or financial advice.

footer.disclaimer: A stylized model using real (inflation-adjusted) returns. An illustration of one mechanism — not a forecast or financial advice.
footer.builtWith: All figures are illustrative anchors; each shows its source.
